title: Problems
summary: This page contains end-of-chapter problems from Chapter 1 covering linear time-invariant systems, causality, memory, and physical system modeling. Problems include analyzing LTI system properties (causality, memorylessness), cascaded system outputs, and deriving differential equations for physical systems such as automobile suspension, field-controlled DC motors, and fluid tanks.

figures.caption: A circuit with a current source input x(t), a 1/2 Ω resistor, a 1 F capacitor, and a 1/2 H inductor connected in parallel, where y1(t) is the voltage across the capacitor and y2(t) is the output voltage across the inductor terminals.
figures.description: The figure shows a parallel RLC circuit driven by an input current source labeled x(t) on the left, with no axes present. A 1/2 ohm resistor, 1 F capacitor, and 1/2 H inductor are connected in parallel between common top and bottom rails; y1(t) is marked across the capacitor branch and y2(t) is the output voltage measured across the right-hand terminals with polarity + at the top and - at the bottom. Students should notice that all three elements share the same node voltage in a parallel network, so the capacitor voltage and output terminal voltage are related to the same top-to-bottom rail voltage.
